title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several infrastructure contracts and discussed ongoing road and utility projects. Key actions included awarding a recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel for $126,900, approving a resolution supporting state transportation funding, and directing staff to develop enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations at $100 per day.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally a 2029 project that will likely move to 2028. The grant covers about 50% of the estimated $1.5 million cost. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading, Schofield Avenue repairs by HOS Construction, and a special assessment meeting scheduled for May 28th for the Bladel Avenue project.

Staff reported road limits were removed April 29th. Work included mailbox and sign repairs, dewatering near Weston Avenue tributary for about a week due to flooding, spring curb sweeping, hot mix patching, storm damage cleanup on East Nick, inlet repairs on Birch Street, and overhead signing on Schofield Avenue. Discussion addressed ongoing flooding issues in the Zenzer area, with staff looking at permanent pump solutions.

Josh reported on water service repairs at Caribou Coffee, well maintenance, and private well sampling. Sanitary side saw significant pump activity due to clear water infiltration. April data showed 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated, indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. Staff discussed the upcoming INI study with In-Pipe Technologies to identify infiltration sources.

Michael reviewed the annual report showing net operating income of approximately $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. Utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure added. The utility serves about 6,100 customers total. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution supports long-term transportation funding solutions. The village has decreased public works budgets recently and seeks state assistance for recurring transportation costs rather than just one-time grants. Motion to recommend adoption passed unanimously.

King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23 per ton for crushing recycled material, with estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. Michael showed this saves approximately $11 per cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, representing about $130,000 in savings across three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on fining residents who discharge clear water into sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day. Committee discussed distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. After discussion, committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100 per day for violations. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael explained a duplex builder on Radkey Avenue needs temporary connection to utilities until the street is reconstructed. Committee added language requiring reconstruction no later than 2029. Motion to recommend approval with the 2029 deadline passed unanimously.
The village will reimburse Rothschild for water and sewer work on Military Road, estimated at $94,860. Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Michael recommended MSA for the Alderson/Jelinek roundabout project at $37,315 and Clark Dietz for the Bladel Avenue project at $73,730, citing preference for experienced full-time staff over interns. Motion to approve both passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton ($126,900 estimated)
Recommend village board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Direct staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations at $100/day
Recommend village board approve Radkey Avenue temporary utility connection agreement with 2029 reconstruction deadline
Recommend village board approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for $94,860
Approve MSA for Alderson/Jelinek roundabout construction services ($37,315)
Approve Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue construction services ($73,730)
Schedule special assessment meeting for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Send flushing notices to residents for water main flushing starting next week
Consider street lighting for Schofield Avenue from Birch to Camp Phillips in 2030 project

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting following reorganization, electing officers and approving a $5,000 grant request for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission noted that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Minutes of the previous meeting - April 21st
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trusty Erling was nominated and elected as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated for Vice Chair, seconded by Jackson, and approved unanimously. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary unanimously.
The minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented on behalf of the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. He reported approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year and announced that a competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commission members questioned room usage, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with a request that the applicant track and report hotel room usage data. Motion passed unanimously.

Renee reported attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting the winner Anastasia had three bachelor's degrees. She highlighted this was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s, and candidates expressed appreciation for the community.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow Balloonfest representatives to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with all commissioners present voting in favor.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Minutes of April 13, 2026 planning commission meeting
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Unfinished business - Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Future agenda items and staff comments

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building was rotated to address challenges with a gas pipeline easement running through the property. Chief Jeremy explained the building will accommodate 50-60 officers (current staffing is 40 officers and 4 clerical). An alternate storage shed is proposed but may be built later depending on budget. Discussion addressed future expansion capability to the north, landscaping meeting current code, and positioning of the alternate shed. Motion to approve was made with conditions 2-5 and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff proposed a new inspection program with two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and drainage, and a winter operational inspection focusing on material handling and containment. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near the village well on Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and commissioners agreed staff should reach out to bring them into compliance. Motion to approve was amended to include reporting to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee, and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
discussions.body: Motion to approve passed unanimously with no discussion.
Motion to approve passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly project update
discussions.body: Staff announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan update is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send email confirmation and calendar invites to commissioners.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, referencing relaxed standards used for the Green Heck Turner building. He also requested the commission receive annual reports on the salt shed inspection program to ensure accountability, noting some zoning complaints have gone unresolved for years.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive approved with conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with twice-annual inspections
Staff to report inspection findings to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee around May 2027
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility on Sternberg that has not completed wellhead protection conditional use process
Joint steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in fall agenda
Next regular meeting scheduled for Monday, June 8th, 2026 at 6:00 p.m.

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new board members, featuring presentations from the Health Department, Veteran Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center. No action items were voted on beyond approving meeting minutes; the meeting focused on introducing department missions, services, budgets, and organizational structures to committee members.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote participation confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - no items presented
Review of standing committee ordinances, missions, duties and responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Office presentation by VSO Erin Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray
Next meeting date discussion and adjournment

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting in favor.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease, promoting health, and protecting from environmental hazards. She highlighted their 37.6 FTE staff, management of over 1,200 licenses, public health vending machines stocked with free resources like tick kits, the Nurse Family Partnership program with first graduates, and a new homeless system response position. Supervisor Kremes asked about pandemic preparedness; Suginaka confirmed they have plans in place, conduct training exercises for measles response, and have a public health emergency preparedness planning body.

Administrator Leonard provided the overview in absence of Director Christa. He described the department's nearly 140 FTE staff, divisions including child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. He highlighted the successful Family Keys pilot program addressing housing instability, noting that of their $22 million budget, approximately $7 million is tax levy with the rest from intergovernmental revenues and grants.

VSO Erin Galindo reported his 3.0 FTE office had 8,149 interactions in 2025, up from 5,900 the previous year. He noted $44 million annually flows to local veterans, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025 alone. Galindo discussed initiatives including surviving spouse benefits, housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention efforts - noting 7 of Marathon County's 30 suicides in 2025 were veterans. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination; Galindo explained state statute requires each county to have a VSO but offices collaborate and can serve any veteran statewide.

Executive Director Mike Ray presented on the four-county ADRC serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties with about 60 FTEs and 385-400 volunteers. He described services including information and assistance, caregiver support, dementia care specialists, and the Meals on Wheels program. Ray highlighted significant financial challenges - nutrition program costs increased 40-45% over four years while federal funding remained flat due to non-reauthorization of the Older Americans Act. The Marathon County levy contribution is $413,135.
Vice Chair Rosenberg caught an error in the announced next meeting date. The meeting was corrected from Wednesday May 29th to Tuesday June 3rd at 3 PM, as May 29th is not a Wednesday.

actionItems: April 1st committee meeting minutes approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with items for future agendas
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan discussion to upcoming meetings
Next meeting scheduled for Tuesday, June 3rd at 3 PM

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed multiple ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market location at Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made appointments to the CLIPS committee.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees
Acknowledgment of reports from boards, committees, and commissions
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances - mobile food vendor, standing committees, nuisances, e-bikes

discussions.item: Park and Recreation Impact Fee Amendment
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park impact fees from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. During public comment, Jim Pitel criticized the lack of detailed cost data justifying the fees. Trustee Barb supported the increase citing population growth and fairness to existing residents. Ordinance 26-010 passed unanimously, and Resolution 2026-015 also passed unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained this ordinance creates a reciprocal inspection agreement between Safer and other municipalities including Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and Clover. Under this system, a food truck inspected by any participating department receives a sticker valid across all jurisdictions, eliminating redundant inspections. Ordinance 26-008 passed unanimously.

The board debated amendments to the remote meeting policy. Trustees agreed to strike language requiring health, safety, or welfare justifications for remote attendance, making the policy more broad. Members must still notify the administrator, clerk, and president 12 hours in advance, keep cameras on, and cannot participate in closed sessions. Trustees Beck and Clark moved and seconded the amendment to remove restrictive language. The amendment passed with Trustees Herling and Motti opposed, and the amended policy passed with the same two opposed.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Herling, second by Lopez, passed unanimously. The board also discussed having committee applications included in board packets going forward.

The board approved a secondhand article dealer and jewelry dealer license for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street, contingent upon satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit. Chief Hunt explained these licenses are required under state statute for businesses buying and selling precious metals to prevent trafficking of stolen merchandise. Passed unanimously as amended.

The board discussed challenges with the farmers market's new location at Kennedy Park after losing their previous site at Camp Phillips and Scoffield Avenue. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about vehicles parking on grass along Alderson Street. Renee explained the market has grown from 25 to 53 vendor spots and vendors appreciate being able to keep vehicles with them. The board acknowledged this is an evolving situation and may require ongoing adjustments. No action was taken as this was discussion only.

Director Michael Dolan reported on the speed study for Weston Avenue. The DOT engineering study said it should be 35 mph, but the 85th percentile speed measured was 45 mph. A second study is needed but was delayed due to construction. The matter will return to the board, possibly at a joint meeting.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke during public comment criticizing the hiring of outside engineering consultants for $108,000 when an engineering technician position remains unfilled. He also criticized the appointment process for committees, noting Lisa Beck was introduced at a Safer meeting before being officially appointed. He called for more transparency and accountability from the administrator. During the park impact fee hearing, Pitel again commented that the village lacks detailed cost data for park maintenance to justify the fee increase.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Comprehensive Plan steering committee first meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement for building inspection assistance to Town of Ringle
Speed study for Weston Avenue to be completed and brought back to board
Remote meeting attendance policy amended with stricter language removed
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license approved for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC pending occupancy and background check
Committee applications to be included in future board packets
Electronics drive scheduled for June 6th (corrected from May 20th)

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the executive committee, and unanimously approved a resolution to reduce landfill tipping fees for debris from the recent tornado and flooding disaster. The board heard emotional testimony from affected Ringle residents requesting fee waivers for disaster cleanup.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Roll call (technical difficulties with meeting manager system)
Committee appointments introduction and Chair Gibbs remarks
Election of at-large executive committee members
Ballot counting for executive committee election
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for disaster relief

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs thanked staff and Vice Chair Boots for assistance in developing committee appointments. He emphasized that 34% of the board is in its first or second term and that appointments were guided by goals including increasing diversity of perspectives, ensuring leadership development, and honoring supervisor preferences. The appointments were approved in a single motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Supervisor Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg spoke in support of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for 'our county's smallest children and our oldest elders.' Paper ballots were distributed for supervisors to select two candidates. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.
Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The consent agenda was approved unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was approved but not unanimously, with no discussion recorded.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was approved but not unanimously, with no discussion recorded.

Administrator Leonard and Director Hagen Busher presented this resolution from the Solid Waste Management Board regarding reduced tipping fees for disaster debris. The county received approximately 400 tons of storm debris, with the proposed fee reduced to $36.94/ton (from $65 standard rate) to cover costs while waiving the state fee ($13.10) and Ringle's fee ($2.33). Leonard noted Marathon County will not meet the $670,743 threshold for state disaster aid, but the town of Ringle likely will. The board suspended rules unanimously to consider this resolution directly. Following public testimony, the resolution was approved unanimously. Motion by Supervisor Arstead, seconded by Supervisor Morash.

publicComment: At Supervisor Rosenberg's request, two residents were granted special permission to speak. Jessica Toasty urged the county to fully waive fees, noting the state and Ringle are waiving their portions, and that private businesses have donated up to $40,000 in cleanup assistance. She stated 'We didn't see anyone from Marathon County' during cleanup and requested fee waivers for six months. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of homeowners facing demolition, noting approximately 44 homes were affected and many are still waiting for insurance adjusters, requesting extended relief time for underinsured residents.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved for the term
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to executive committee as at-large members
Resolution 24-26 approved for out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26 approved for social services budget amendment
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to approximately $36.94/ton for certified disaster debris through June 17th
Administrator Leonard to work with Community Foundation on potential reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop clear policy within six months for handling similar disaster situations in the future
Board to revisit tipping fee extension at May meeting if needed

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board to help tornado victims. The committee also received educational presentations from three county departments covering solid waste operations, parks and forestry services, and conservation planning and zoning.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (conservancy recreation to general agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Department overview: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry
Department overview: Conservation, Planning, and Zoning

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Town of Green Valley)
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for a proposed land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered for, against, or as interested. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Town of McMillan)
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the request to rezone from conservancy recreation to general agriculture. Supervisor Lever asked about the conservancy nature and whether natural areas would be lost. Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and the rezone would make the non-conforming home use conforming. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve and forward to county board, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather victims
discussions.body: Director Lori Miskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged in the April 17th tornado affecting the Town of Ringle, Village of Kronenwetter, Town of Easton, and Village of Weston. County Administrator Lance Leonhard noted this was triggered by the governor's emergency declaration. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about fairness to individuals hit by disasters without emergency declarations. Marilyn Bend asked about septic hookup costs. After extensive debate, Supervisor Drebeck proposed an amendment to limit the waiver to only reconnect fees for septic systems ($160 each), excluding other zoning and septic fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor Kurt and passed. The amended resolution was then approved to forward to county board, though not unanimously.

David Hagenbusher presented on Solid Waste operations, covering the landfill's 45-year history, renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, and upcoming leachate treatment requirements by 2032. Tom Lovelin presented Parks, Recreation, and Forestry overview, noting the department serves both county and city of Wausau with 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees. Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld acknowledged Tom Lovelin's retirement effective June 1st after nearly 30 years of service.

actionItems: Forward Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential, Town of Green Valley) to Marathon County Board with approval recommendation
Forward Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (conservancy recreation to general agriculture, Town of McMillan) to Marathon County Board with approval recommendation
Forward amended fee waiver resolution to county board - limited to waiving only septic reconnect fees ($160) for properties damaged in April 17, 2026 severe weather
CPZ staff to track costs and requests related to any fee waivers for future policy consideration
Conservation services presentation to be scheduled for future summer meeting

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center to next month due to the chair's absence, approved installation of new kayak launch signs along the Plover River, and received updates on Kennedy Park development and Yellow Banks disc golf course improvements. Public commenters criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time and questioned Kennedy Park fundraising transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on Oaklair River Trail discussion
Topics for future meetings and staff remarks

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27, 2026 meeting were approved unanimously with a motion and second. No discussion was held on the minutes.

Staff presented a detailed timeline of the Kennedy Park master plan development from approval in November 2023 through the May 9th ribbon cutting for baseball fields. Scott asked clarifying questions about village funding versus Friends of Kennedy Park fundraising responsibilities. Sean Osterbrink explained the Friends group is pursuing smaller fundraising goals, currently targeting $117,000 for field lights with approximately $84-85,000 already at the community foundation. The Friends group plans a public check presentation at their June tournament. Luis emphasized the Friends group deserves grace as volunteers and encouraged community members to get involved.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Dan presented photos and a PDF showing the location of trees removed from the river and the installation of wood mats over gas pipelines to protect them during equipment access. The mats were described as approximately 8x8 lumber with notches and lifting points. TC Energy (formerly ANR pipeline) representatives visited the site during the work and conducted a walkthrough afterward.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to next month because Chair Katrina was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A resident named Andy (6504 [inaudible] Street) spoke during public comment opposing dogs at the pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' His objection was noted for the record.

Sean explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and benches on the trail, noting sandblasters require pressure washers that cannot access the area due to bridge weight limits and terrain. The committee discussed possible solutions including anti-vandalism coatings and volunteer work days. Staff will investigate options for cleaning and potentially sealing surfaces. No formal action was taken.

Dan Girolamo from 2625 Northwestern Avenue presented version 7 of kayak launch signs for the Plover River waterway. Three signs will be installed in Weston (at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks). The signs are funded through Marathon County Parks Foundation and state waterway grants at no cost to the village. Dan noted the Highway J launch may be closed all season due to bridge replacement. Motion to approve sign installation passed unanimously.

Sean raised a request from a community member about installing food allergy awareness signage on playgrounds. The committee agreed to bring this back for future discussion. Staff also announced the Mock Miller Park master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler, and the final bike and pedestrian plan open house is June 11th from 5-7 p.m.

publicComment: Two people spoke during public comment. Andy (6504 [inaudible] Street) spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating dogs belong in rivers and lakes, not public pools. Jim Pensan (5002 [inaudible] Street, appearing online under the name 'Vern M') criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned Kennedy Park financial reporting, calling the $8,700 figure for equipment 'too coincidental' and demanding to know where grant money and Glow Games fundraising revenue are accounted for.
actionItems: Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to investigate graffiti removal options and anti-vandalism coatings for Oaklair River Trail
Approved installation of kayak launch signs at Yellow Banks, Ross Avenue, and Highway J locations
Staff to bring food allergy awareness playground signage proposal to future meeting
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged monthly financial reports. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam on the clerk's office operations, including elections, licensing, tax collection, and the village newsletter. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving out of Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's office and budget
Staff remarks and farmers market update

discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes from the previous Finance and HR meeting was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. The motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

Jessica provided an update on TIF closure, stating they are anticipating October/November for final bond payoff. She explained the CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, which are due October 1st. A committee member asked about final numbers, and Jessica confirmed they will be able to pay off the debt.
The April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report were acknowledged together. Motion to acknowledge was made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper, and passed unanimously.

Clerk Pam delivered an extensive presentation covering the clerk's office staffing (herself and Sarah full-time, Leah part-time), election administration including Badger Books electronic poll books costing about $11,000 for four units, licensing operations covering 51 liquor licenses, and tax collection where 84% ($22 million) is collected at the clerk's office counter. She noted the office went from four full-time staff to three, and handled the entire 2024 presidential election with just two people. Discussion included pet licensing fee increases from $10 to $20 due to Marathon County raising fees, and the cost of the village newsletter (approximately $30,000 annually for postage and printing). A committee member questioned the $175 per mouse cost under the non-dog stray contract with Mountain Bay Metro.

Pam presented survey data from 52 Marathon County municipalities showing Weston pays $12/hour for election inspectors (average) but is on the lower end for chief inspectors. She indicated she will likely propose increasing chief inspector pay to $15/hour for the next two-year term due to their increased responsibilities.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market has been going well with her attending the last three Saturdays from 6 a.m. for 6-7 hours. The market has expanded to include coffee vendors, new food trucks, and craft vendors. Produce vendors will increase mid to late June. Discussion addressed parking concerns on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff clarifying the east side of Alderson is signed no parking while the west side now allows parking.

Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. He thanked the committee for his time serving. The chair noted the committee now has open spots and encouraged members to reach out to citizens interested in participating.

publicComment: No public comment was offered. The chair asked for comments from the audience and online, with none coming forward.
actionItems: CDA to pass resolution to call TIF bonds, anticipated this summer before October 1st due date
Contact Ellers to write letter to US Bank Associate Trust regarding bond call
Recruit citizens to fill open committee positions
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 p.m.

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th, taking action on several items including recommending approval of nuisance ordinance amendments, an e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions, mobile food vendor fire inspection requirements, address corrections, and a raze order for an abandoned property. The committee also discussed overnight winter parking regulations but deferred that decision for further staff analysis.

agenda.item: Call to order and pledge of allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Written communications and department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Request for additional crosswalk on Alderson Street
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment

discussions.body: Roman/Audrey from code enforcement noted some enforcement-suspended cases may move back to pending as construction season begins. She announced 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement role after the property sold at auction and the new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work summer zoning violations for Weston, Rothschild, and Schofield. Motion to acknowledge reports passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments to Chapter 50, including previously discussed changes to tree limb heights over streets and walkways (switched to 14 and 10 feet) with language requiring compliance with the jurisdiction having authority. Tolson confirmed no additional changes were needed. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

Discussion centered on whether to allow emotos (electric motorcycles/dirt bikes) in the village. Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting emotos on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes and citing safety concerns including people riding wheelies past pedestrians. Committee member Chwanka agreed emotos should be off roadways. Discussion also addressed electric scooters, with Campkey noting many middle schoolers use them. The committee decided to leave officer discretion for kids' play scooters. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend approval with emotos prohibited from roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property passed unanimously.

Tolson initiated discussion noting Weston's 12-hour overnight parking window (4am-4pm, October 31-May 1, even/odd basis) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities, which average 5-hour windows. Director Michael Wodalski suggested potentially eliminating overnight parking restrictions entirely except for signing 6-8 problem areas, noting plowing rarely occurs before 4-5am. Chief Hunt opposed elimination, preferring uniform overnight restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before morning plowing. The committee agreed to defer the item, directing Wodalski to identify specific problem areas and bring recommendations back in July/August.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest school parking lot. Staff estimated basic crosswalk cost at approximately $2,000, lighted system at $25-30,000. Committee members expressed reluctance to pay for crosswalks when requested by private entities. Chief Hunt reported no known incidents at the location. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend board approval contingent on Mount Olive covering installation costs passed unanimously. Wodalski will contact Mount Olive regarding cost-sharing.

Chief Hunt explained a new regional fire inspection program involving Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and potentially Weston. Under the program, any participating department can inspect food trucks, with a sticker indicating compliance valid across all municipalities. Wausau opted out to do their own inspections. Fire Marshal Marty has already begun inspections, with one vendor failing due to galvanized steel LP piping and improperly secured propane containers. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Campkey, to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Jen Higgins presented three address corrections for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicowski Street (duplex needing separate addresses, owners agreed), Ross Avenue addresses out of sequence near an apartment building, and 2211 Radkey Avenue for a new duplex on a private drive. Chief Hunt added there's an eightplex on Delicowski (Weston Villa Apartments) where each unit has a separate address that may also need review. Motion passed unanimously to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees.

Staff presented a raze request for a structure abandoned since 2010-2011 that has been posted for several years. A planned fire department training burn was cancelled because the structure was too deteriorated for safe exercise. Staff discovered chickens had been housed inside during winter. The property owners, who operate a small farm and are current on taxes, prefer the village proceed with the raze order. Demolition costs will be placed on the tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding several agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location, predicting grass damage from vehicle parking and questioning whether current code prohibits parking on grass. He noted ADA parking spaces were placed on grass rather than adjacent asphalt, which he argued doesn't meet ADA requirements for firm, level surfaces. He questioned the origin of new food truck fire inspection requirements. He also raised concerns about mobile food vendor permitting on public property and ambiguity about which days constitute 'farmers market days.' Finally, he criticized the code enforcement report, noting some cases have been pending for years with no visible progress and requested periodic updates on older cases.
actionItems: Recommend approval of Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees
Recommend approval of e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions to Board of Trustees
Michael Wodalski to identify 6-8 problem areas for specific winter parking signage and bring back to committee in July/August
Michael Wodalski to contact Mount Olive Church regarding cost-sharing for crosswalk installation
Approve Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections
Recommend address corrections to Board of Trustees for E911 compliance
Authorize staff to begin raze order process for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property on Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster in yard

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations

Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
